label: hot_take

text:
Approval rating means so little when people don't bother to vote. Trump won his second term with a voter turnout of 64% so (slightly) more than a third of the people able to vote didn't bother to do so.

If 100 people can vote, 40 of them approve Trump and 60 don't, depending on how the 64% voter turnout splits you can get widely different results.

In this example 64% means that only 64 people voted, if you split those 100-0 in either way you either get 40 votes for Trump, 24 against or 60 votes against and 4 votes for Trump. In reality the split wouldn't be that ridiculous but it's just to prove a point.

And the upcoming midterms are going to have the same issue. Midterm turnout is about 15% lower than presidential election turnout so it's going to be around 50% if the trend follows. 

I just wish people all over the world, not just the US, cared enough to vote because this situation is universal.
label: hot_take

text: People go out and vote when they have someone to vote for
label: hot_take

text:
meh, it's less people voting, it's the seriousness, involvement/connectedness/education/whatever of the voting population. The last election was the second highest turnout since 1900, with the one before being the highest by a couple points. not saying turnout isn't important and voting 'accessibility' is something we dramatically need to address.

Practically every incumbent administration around the world faced a defeat this last round regardless of political direction as almost always happens in economic downturns. Then couple in the emotional attraction of a populist, years of emotional messaging against our establishment / historic institutions / globalism / omgdangers! and then another significant blunder by the democrats to basically dissuade

The shifts we saw certainly don't represent long-term held beliefs that align with 'maga' just really common disenfranchisement that we've seen around the world in various intensities. and really unserious political involvement.

text: "Challenge" could be somewhat ambiguous but the guidance that IFAB gives to refs on interference focuses heavily on actual impact. Intent of the law is definitely not to define running toward the ball as interference. They want to see an obvious impact on the opponent to chalk a goal off. Balogun doesn't get there where he physically impacts keeper. You could argue he throws him off but now they generally try to avoid calling that speculatively. Has to be really obvious.
label: analysis

text:
No. He doesn't play the ball, he's to the keeper's right, not blocking vision, has zero impact on the keeper not saving it.

People do this every time an offside player is anywhere near the play, but the bar for 'being involved in play' for the purposes of offside is very specific and this play doesn't meet the criteria.
label: analysis

text: I feel like it's reasonable to say he impacted the run of the defender? I don't know though the more I watch the more I'm surprised it was overturned
label: analysis

text: The defender that is 3 yards behind the play and has no chance of challenging for the ball, and that Balogun doesn't make any contact with? Tough sell.
label: analysis

text: I'd say it really looks like the defender hesitated in that situation, partially because he was blocked by the offsides player
label: analysis

text: He probably should have at least made contact with the offsides player then, but he wasn't even close enough to the play to do that. There is no physical universe where the defender is able to make a legitimate play on the ball regardless if Balogun is there or not. Even if he dove headfirst and arm tackled Freeman from behind I doubt he gets there or stops the header. Even if by some miracle he does its a red and PK.
label: analysis

text: If you read ifab 11.2 “interfering with an opponent” there are 3 points that could matter here. One is “challenging an opponent for the ball”, I’d argue him initially sprinting towards the ball is him challenging the opponent. Next, “clearly attempting to play a ball which is close when this action impacts on an opponent” again I think it could apply for the reason above. Next, “making an obvious action which clearly impacts on the ability of an opponent to play the ball”, I think this one applies less because there isn’t exactly a single action that does stop the defenders from clearing the ball.
label: analysis

text: Because he had zero impact on the play, him attempting to play the ball isn’t relevant if there’s no impact on the play and there clearly wasn’t.
label: analysis
